notes.title: Units and Measurements — Complete JEE Notes
notes.description:
## Overview
Every physical quantity = **magnitude × unit**. This chapter builds the language of physics: units, dimensions, error analysis and significant figures. It is scoring and conceptual.

## 1. Physical Quantities & Units
- **Fundamental (base) quantities (7):** Length (metre, m), Mass (kilogram, kg), Time (second, s), Electric current (ampere, A), Temperature (kelvin, K), Amount of substance (mole, mol), Luminous intensity (candela, cd).
- **Supplementary units:** plane angle (radian, rad), solid angle (steradian, sr).
- **Derived units:** obtained from base units, e.g. speed (m/s), force (newton = kg·m/s²), energy (joule = kg·m²/s²).
- **System of units:** CGS, FPS, MKS, and the modern **SI** system.

## 2. Dimensions & Dimensional Analysis
- **Dimensional formula** expresses a quantity using [M], [L], [T], [A], [K], [mol], [cd].
- Examples: Force [M¹L¹T⁻²], Work/Energy [M¹L²T⁻²], Power [M¹L²T⁻³], Pressure [M¹L⁻¹T⁻²], Momentum [M¹L¹T⁻¹].
- **Uses:** (a) check correctness of an equation (principle of homogeneity — both sides must have same dimensions), (b) convert units from one system to another, (c) derive relations between quantities.
- **Limitations:** cannot find dimensionless constants (like ½, 2π), cannot derive relations with more than 3 unknowns, fails for trigonometric/exponential/log functions.

## 3. Significant Figures
- All non-zero digits are significant. Zeros between non-zero digits are significant. Leading zeros are NOT significant. Trailing zeros after a decimal ARE significant.
- **In addition/subtraction:** result has as many decimal places as the term with the least decimal places.
- **In multiplication/division:** result has as many significant figures as the term with the least significant figures.

## 4. Errors in Measurement
- **Absolute error** = |measured − true|. **Mean absolute error** = average of absolute errors.
- **Relative error** = mean absolute error / mean value. **Percentage error** = relative error × 100.
- **Combination of errors:**
  - Sum/Difference (Z = A ± B): absolute errors add → ΔZ = ΔA + ΔB.
  - Product/Quotient (Z = AB or A/B): relative errors add → ΔZ/Z = ΔA/A + ΔB/B.
  - Power (Z = AᵖBᵠ/Cʳ): ΔZ/Z = p(ΔA/A) + q(ΔB/B) + r(ΔC/C).

## 5. Key Formulas
- Percentage error = (ΔZ/Z) × 100
- For Z = (Aᵖ Bᵠ)/Cʳ → %error = p·%A + q·%B + r·%C

## ⚠️ Common Traps
- Powers MAGNIFY errors — a quantity raised to power 3 contributes 3× its error.
- Dimensionally correct ≠ physically correct (constants are missed).
- Radians and angles are dimensionless.

## 📝 Quick Recap
- 7 base quantities; learn dimensional formulas of mechanics quantities.
- Homogeneity principle to verify equations.
- Errors: add absolutes for ±, add relatives for ×/÷, multiply by power.

## 🎯 Exam Tip
Most questions ask for **percentage error in a derived quantity** — just multiply each term's %error by its power and add.

notes.title: Motion in a Straight Line — Complete JEE Notes
notes.description:
## Overview
1-D kinematics: describing motion along a line using position, velocity and acceleration, including graphical analysis and free fall.

## 1. Basic Terms
- **Distance** (scalar): total path length, always ≥ 0. **Displacement** (vector): change in position, can be 0 or negative.
- **Speed** (scalar) vs **Velocity** (vector). Average speed = total distance/total time; Average velocity = displacement/time.
- **Instantaneous velocity** = dx/dt; **Instantaneous acceleration** = dv/dt = d²x/dt².
- |Average velocity| ≤ Average speed (equal only for straight-line motion without reversal).

## 2. Uniformly Accelerated Motion (a = constant)
- v = u + at
- s = ut + ½at²
- v² = u² + 2as
- Distance in nth second: sₙ = u + a(n − ½)
- If u = 0: distances in successive seconds are in ratio 1 : 3 : 5 : 7 … (odd numbers).

## 3. Motion Under Gravity (free fall)
- Take a = g ≈ 9.8 m/s² (downward positive when falling).
- For a body thrown up with speed u: time to top = u/g; max height = u²/2g; total time of flight = 2u/g.
- Velocity at highest point = 0, but acceleration = g (never zero).

## 4. Graphs (very important for MCQs)
- **x–t graph:** slope = velocity. Straight line → uniform velocity; curve → acceleration.
- **v–t graph:** slope = acceleration; **area under curve = displacement**.
- **a–t graph:** area under curve = change in velocity.

## 5. Relative Velocity
- v(AB) = v(A) − v(B). For same direction subtract speeds, for opposite direction add.
- Used in river-boat and rain-man problems.

## ⚠️ Common Traps
- Displacement can be zero even if distance is large (round trip).
- At the highest point of vertical throw, a = g (NOT zero).
- Average velocity ≠ average of velocities unless acceleration is uniform.

## 📝 Quick Recap
- Three equations of motion (constant a).
- Free-fall: symmetric up/down times, v = 0 at top.
- v–t slope = a, area = displacement.

## 🎯 Exam Tip
For graph questions, read SLOPE and AREA — they instantly give acceleration and displacement.

notes.title: Motion in a Plane — Complete JEE Notes
notes.description:
## Overview
2-D motion using vectors — vector algebra, projectile motion and uniform circular motion.

## 1. Vectors
- **Scalar** (magnitude only) vs **Vector** (magnitude + direction).
- **Addition:** Triangle law, Parallelogram law. Resultant of P and Q: R = √(P² + Q² + 2PQ cosθ); direction tanα = Q sinθ /(P + Q cosθ).
- **Resolution:** Aₓ = A cosθ, A_y = A sinθ; A = √(Aₓ² + A_y²).
- **Dot product:** A·B = AB cosθ (scalar). **Cross product:** A×B = AB sinθ n̂ (vector, ⟂ to both).
- Unit vector  = A/|A|.

## 2. Projectile Motion (launched at angle θ)
- Horizontal: uniform velocity u cosθ. Vertical: uniform acceleration −g.
- **Time of flight:** T = 2u sinθ/g
- **Maximum height:** H = u²sin²θ/2g
- **Horizontal range:** R = u²sin2θ/g (maximum at θ = 45°)
- Range is equal for complementary angles (θ and 90°−θ).
- Path is a **parabola**. At max height vertical velocity = 0, horizontal velocity = u cosθ.

## 3. Projectile from a Height (horizontal projection)
- Time to fall: t = √(2h/g); Horizontal range: x = u√(2h/g).

## 4. Uniform Circular Motion
- Speed constant, velocity (direction) changes → motion is accelerated.
- **Angular velocity** ω = θ/t = 2π/T = 2πf; v = rω.
- **Centripetal acceleration:** a꜀ = v²/r = ω²r, directed toward centre.

## ⚠️ Common Traps
- A projectile's horizontal velocity NEVER changes (no horizontal force).
- In UCM speed is constant but the body is still ACCELERATING (direction changes).
- Two vectors of equal magnitude can give zero resultant only if antiparallel.

## 📝 Quick Recap
- R max at 45°; complementary angles give same range.
- a꜀ = v²/r toward centre.
- Resolve vectors before applying equations.

## 🎯 Exam Tip
For 'maximum range' or 'same range' questions, remember θ and (90°−θ) give identical range.

notes.title: Laws of Motion — Complete JEE Notes
notes.description:
## Overview
Newton's laws relate force and motion. Master free-body diagrams (FBD), friction and circular dynamics — high-weightage chapter.

## 1. Newton's Three Laws
- **First law (Inertia):** a body continues in rest/uniform motion unless a net external force acts. Defines inertia and force.
- **Second law:** F = dp/dt = ma (p = momentum). Direction of acceleration = direction of net force.
- **Third law:** action and reaction are equal, opposite, and act on DIFFERENT bodies.

## 2. Momentum & Impulse
- **Linear momentum** p = mv.
- **Impulse** J = F·Δt = Δp (area under F–t graph). Large force for short time = impulse (e.g. bat hitting ball).
- **Conservation of momentum:** if net external force = 0, total momentum is conserved. Basis of recoil of gun, rocket propulsion.

## 3. Friction
- **Static friction** (self-adjusting): f ≤ μₛN; maximum = limiting friction μₛN.
- **Kinetic friction:** f = μₖN (μₖ < μₛ).
- **Angle of friction** tanθ = μ; **Angle of repose** = angle at which body just slides (equals angle of friction).
- Friction is independent of area of contact, depends on nature of surfaces.

## 4. Connected Bodies & Pulleys
- Apply F = ma to each body separately using FBD; tension is internal.
- Two masses over a frictionless pulley: a = (m₁−m₂)g/(m₁+m₂), T = 2m₁m₂g/(m₁+m₂).

## 5. Circular Motion Dynamics
- Centripetal force F = mv²/r = mω²r (real force: tension, gravity, friction).
- **Banking of roads:** ideal speed v = √(rg tanθ); with friction v_max = √[rg(μ+tanθ)/(1−μtanθ)].
- **Vertical circle:** minimum speed at top = √(gr); at bottom = √(5gr).

## ⚠️ Common Traps
- Action–reaction never cancel (they act on different bodies).
- 'Centrifugal force' is pseudo (only in rotating frame).
- Static friction is NOT always μₛN — that's only its maximum value.

## 📝 Quick Recap
- F = ma; impulse = change in momentum.
- μₛ > μₖ; angle of repose = angle of friction.
- Vertical circle: v_top(min) = √(gr).

## 🎯 Exam Tip
Always draw the FBD first and resolve forces along/perpendicular to motion — most numerical mistakes come from skipping this.

notes.title: Work, Energy and Power — Complete JEE Notes
notes.description:
## Overview
Energy approach to mechanics — work, kinetic & potential energy, conservation and collisions.

## 1. Work
- **Work** W = F·s = Fs cosθ (scalar). Unit: joule (J).
- Positive work (0°≤θ<90°), zero work (θ=90°, e.g. centripetal force), negative work (90°<θ≤180°, e.g. friction).
- **Work by a variable force** = area under F–x graph = ∫F dx.

## 2. Energy
- **Kinetic energy** KE = ½mv² = p²/2m.
- **Work–Energy Theorem:** net work done = change in KE (W_net = ½mv² − ½mu²).
- **Potential energy:** gravitational U = mgh; elastic (spring) U = ½kx².
- **Conservative force:** work depends only on end points (gravity, spring); work in a closed loop = 0. **Non-conservative:** friction, viscosity.

## 3. Conservation of Mechanical Energy
- If only conservative forces act, KE + PE = constant.
- Free fall example: at any height, ½mv² + mgh = constant.

## 4. Power
- **Power** = rate of doing work. P_avg = W/t; P_inst = F·v = Fv cosθ. Unit: watt (W). 1 hp = 746 W.

## 5. Collisions
- **Momentum is ALWAYS conserved** in collisions (no external force).
- **Elastic collision:** KE also conserved. For equal masses, velocities are exchanged. 1-D elastic: v₁ = ((m₁−m₂)u₁ + 2m₂u₂)/(m₁+m₂).
- **Inelastic collision:** KE not conserved; perfectly inelastic → bodies stick together.
- **Coefficient of restitution** e = (v₂−v₁)/(u₁−u₂); e = 1 (elastic), 0 (perfectly inelastic), 0<e<1 (real).

## Key Formulas
- W = Fs cosθ, KE = ½mv² = p²/2m, P = Fv
- Spring PE = ½kx²; e = relative velocity of separation / approach

## ⚠️ Common Traps
- Work done by centripetal force = 0 (perpendicular to velocity).
- In ALL collisions momentum is conserved, but KE only in elastic ones.
- A spring stores the same PE whether stretched or compressed by x.

## 📝 Quick Recap
- Work–energy theorem links force and speed change.
- Equal-mass elastic collision → velocity exchange.
- e = 1 elastic, e = 0 perfectly inelastic.

## 🎯 Exam Tip
Use energy conservation (not equations of motion) when the question involves speed at different heights — it's faster.

notes.chapter: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
notes.title: System of Particles & Rotational Motion — Complete JEE Notes
notes.description:
## Overview
Extends mechanics to rigid bodies: centre of mass, torque, moment of inertia, angular momentum and rolling.

## 1. Centre of Mass (COM)
- COM: x_cm = Σmᵢxᵢ / Σmᵢ. For uniform symmetric bodies, COM is at the geometric centre.
- COM moves as if total mass and net external force act there; internal forces don't move COM.

## 2. Torque & Equilibrium
- **Torque** τ = r × F = rF sinθ (turning effect). Unit: N·m.
- **Couple:** two equal opposite forces; produces pure rotation.
- Conditions of equilibrium: ΣF = 0 (translational) AND Στ = 0 (rotational).

## 3. Moment of Inertia (I)
- Rotational analogue of mass: I = Σmᵢrᵢ². Depends on mass, its distribution and axis.
- **Radius of gyration** k: I = Mk².
- **Theorems:** Parallel axis I = I_cm + Md²; Perpendicular axis (for laminae) I_z = I_x + I_y.
- Standard values: ring (MR²), disc (½MR²), solid sphere (2/5 MR²), hollow sphere (2/3 MR²), rod about centre (1/12 ML²).

## 4. Rotational Dynamics
- τ = Iα (analogue of F = ma).
- **Angular momentum** L = Iω = r × p. **Conservation of L:** if net external torque = 0, Iω = constant (spinning skater).
- **Rotational KE** = ½Iω².

## 5. Rolling Motion
- Rolling = translation of COM + rotation. v_cm = ωR (rolling without slipping).
- Total KE = ½mv² + ½Iω² = ½mv²(1 + k²/R²).
- On an incline, acceleration a = g sinθ /(1 + k²/R²); a sphere reaches bottom before a ring.

## ⚠️ Common Traps
- I depends on the AXIS — same body has different I about different axes.
- For conservation of L, when I decreases ω increases (and KE increases!).
- Hollow bodies have larger I than solid ones of same mass/radius.

## 📝 Quick Recap
- τ = Iα, L = Iω (conserved if τ_ext = 0).
- Parallel axis theorem I = I_cm + Md².
- Rolling KE has both translational and rotational parts.

## 🎯 Exam Tip
For 'which body rolls down fastest', smaller k²/R² wins → solid sphere > disc > hollow sphere > ring.

notes.title: Gravitation — Complete JEE Notes
notes.description:
## Overview
Universal gravitation, variation of g, gravitational potential/energy, and satellite motion.

## 1. Newton's Universal Law
- F = Gm₁m₂/r²; G = 6.67×10⁻¹¹ N·m²/kg² (universal constant).
- Gravitational force is always attractive and acts along the line joining the masses.

## 2. Acceleration due to Gravity (g)
- At surface: g = GM/R².
- **With height h:** g_h = g(1 − 2h/R) for h ≪ R.
- **With depth d:** g_d = g(1 − d/R); g = 0 at the centre of Earth.
- **With latitude / rotation:** g is maximum at poles, minimum at equator.

## 3. Gravitational Field, Potential & PE
- **Field** E = F/m = GM/r² (toward mass).
- **Potential** V = −GM/r (always negative; zero at infinity).
- **Potential energy** U = −Gm₁m₂/r.

## 4. Satellites
- **Orbital velocity** v₀ = √(GM/r) = √(gR²/r); near surface ≈ 7.9 km/s.
- **Time period** T = 2π√(r³/GM) → Kepler's third law T² ∝ r³.
- **Geostationary satellite:** T = 24 h, height ≈ 36,000 km, equatorial orbit.
- **Escape velocity** vₑ = √(2GM/R) = √(2gR) ≈ 11.2 km/s; vₑ = √2 × v₀.
- **Total energy of satellite** E = −GMm/2r (negative → bound). KE = +GMm/2r, PE = −GMm/r.

## 5. Kepler's Laws
- Law of orbits (ellipse), Law of areas (equal areas in equal times → angular momentum conserved), Law of periods (T² ∝ a³).

## ⚠️ Common Traps
- g decreases with BOTH height and depth, but by different factors (2h/R vs d/R).
- Escape velocity is independent of mass and angle of projection.
- A satellite's total energy is negative (bound system).

## 📝 Quick Recap
- g = GM/R²; vₑ = √2·v₀ ≈ 11.2 km/s.
- T² ∝ r³ (Kepler).
- Potential and PE are negative.

## 🎯 Exam Tip
Remember vₑ = √2 × v₀ — many MCQs just test this relationship.

notes.title: Mechanical Properties of Solids — Complete JEE Notes
notes.description:
## Overview
Elastic behaviour of solids: stress, strain, moduli of elasticity and energy stored.

## 1. Stress & Strain
- **Stress** = restoring force / area (N/m² = Pa). Types: tensile, compressive, shearing, volumetric.
- **Strain** = change/original dimension (dimensionless). Types: longitudinal (ΔL/L), shear (Δx/L), volumetric (ΔV/V).

## 2. Hooke's Law & Stress–Strain Curve
- Within elastic limit, stress ∝ strain → stress = E × strain.
- Curve features: proportional limit, elastic limit, **yield point**, plastic region, **breaking/fracture point**. **Elastomers** (rubber) show no straight-line region.

## 3. Moduli of Elasticity
- **Young's modulus** Y = longitudinal stress/longitudinal strain = FL/(AΔL).
- **Bulk modulus** K = −P/(ΔV/V); compressibility = 1/K.
- **Shear/Rigidity modulus** η = shearing stress/shearing strain.
- **Poisson's ratio** σ = lateral strain/longitudinal strain (0 to 0.5).

## 4. Elastic Potential Energy
- Energy stored per unit volume = ½ × stress × strain = ½ × Y × strain².
- Total energy in a wire = ½ × F × ΔL.

## ⚠️ Common Traps
- Higher Young's modulus = MORE elastic (resists deformation more) — steel > rubber.
- Strain is dimensionless; stress has the same unit as pressure.
- Bulk modulus applies to volume change; rigidity to shape change.

## 📝 Quick Recap
- Y = FL/(AΔL); within elastic limit Hooke's law holds.
- Energy density = ½ stress × strain.
- Poisson's ratio lies between 0 and 0.5.

## 🎯 Exam Tip
For 'extension of wire' problems use ΔL = FL/(AY) — thicker/shorter wires stretch less.

notes.title: Mechanical Properties of Fluids — Complete JEE Notes
notes.description:
## Overview
Fluids at rest and in motion: pressure, Pascal & Archimedes, viscosity, surface tension and Bernoulli.

## 1. Pressure in Fluids
- Pressure P = F/A; at depth h: P = P₀ + ρgh (hydrostatic). Pressure acts equally in all directions.
- **Pascal's law:** pressure applied to an enclosed fluid is transmitted undiminished — basis of hydraulic lift/brakes (F₂ = F₁·A₂/A₁).

## 2. Buoyancy (Archimedes' Principle)
- Upthrust = weight of fluid displaced = ρ_fluid·V_submerged·g.
- Floating: weight = upthrust. Fraction submerged = ρ_body/ρ_fluid.

## 3. Viscosity
- Internal friction between fluid layers: F = ηA(dv/dx); η = coefficient of viscosity (Pa·s).
- **Stokes' law:** F = 6πηrv (sphere in fluid).
- **Terminal velocity:** v_t = 2r²(ρ−σ)g/9η (constant velocity when net force = 0).
- **Reynolds number** Re decides laminar (Re<2000) vs turbulent (Re>3000) flow.

## 4. Surface Tension
- **Surface tension** T = force/length = surface energy/area (N/m). Caused by cohesive forces.
- **Excess pressure:** drop = 2T/r; soap bubble = 4T/r (two surfaces); air bubble in liquid = 2T/r.
- **Capillary rise** h = 2T cosθ/(rρg). Angle of contact θ < 90° (wetting) → rise; θ > 90° → depression (mercury).

## 5. Fluid Dynamics — Bernoulli's Principle
- Equation of continuity: A₁v₁ = A₂v₂ (mass conservation).
- **Bernoulli:** P + ½ρv² + ρgh = constant (energy conservation). Faster flow → lower pressure.
- Applications: aeroplane lift, atomizer, Venturimeter, Magnus effect.

## ⚠️ Common Traps
- Soap bubble has TWO surfaces → excess pressure 4T/r (double a drop).
- Terminal velocity ∝ r² (bigger drops fall faster).
- Pressure depends on depth, NOT on shape/amount of liquid.

## 📝 Quick Recap
- P = P₀ + ρgh; Pascal & hydraulic lift.
- v_t = 2r²(ρ−σ)g/9η.
- Bernoulli: high speed = low pressure.

## 🎯 Exam Tip
For capillary/excess-pressure MCQs, remember the factor: drop 2T/r, soap bubble 4T/r.

notes.title: Thermal Properties of Matter — Complete JEE Notes
notes.description:
## Overview
Temperature, thermal expansion, heat capacity, calorimetry and the three modes of heat transfer.

## 1. Temperature & Scales
- Conversion: C/5 = (F−32)/9 = (K−273.15)/5.
- Absolute zero = 0 K = −273.15°C.

## 2. Thermal Expansion
- Linear: ΔL = LαΔT; Area: ΔA = AβΔT; Volume: ΔV = VγΔT.
- Relation: α : β : γ = 1 : 2 : 3.
- **Anomalous expansion of water:** contracts from 0→4°C (max density at 4°C) — lets aquatic life survive.

## 3. Heat & Calorimetry
- **Specific heat** c: Q = mcΔT. **Molar specific heat** C = Mc.
- **Latent heat:** Q = mL (L_fusion of ice = 80 cal/g; L_vaporization of water = 540 cal/g).
- **Principle of calorimetry:** heat lost by hot body = heat gained by cold body (no loss).

## 4. Heat Transfer
- **Conduction:** rate = KA(ΔT)/L (solids). K = thermal conductivity.
- **Convection:** by actual movement of fluid (land/sea breeze).
- **Radiation:** no medium needed; **Stefan–Boltzmann law** E = σT⁴ (for black body), e σT⁴ for real body.
- **Newton's law of cooling:** rate of cooling ∝ (T − T_surrounding), valid for small temperature difference.
- **Wien's displacement law:** λ_max·T = b (hotter bodies emit shorter wavelengths).

## ⚠️ Common Traps
- Water expands when cooled from 4°C to 0°C (anomalous).
- Latent heat changes state at constant temperature (Q is large).
- E ∝ T⁴ — doubling absolute temperature increases radiation 16×.

## 📝 Quick Recap
- α:β:γ = 1:2:3; max density of water at 4°C.
- Q = mcΔT and Q = mL.
- Stefan: E = σT⁴; Newton's cooling for small ΔT.

## 🎯 Exam Tip
Use absolute (Kelvin) temperature in radiation problems — T⁴ makes small changes huge.

notes.title: Thermodynamics — Complete JEE Notes
notes.description:
## Overview
Laws governing heat, work and internal energy; thermodynamic processes and heat engines.

## 1. Basic Terms
- **System, surroundings, boundary.** **Internal energy U** is a state function (depends only on state, not path).
- Heat Q and Work W are path functions.
- Sign convention: Q positive when added to system; W positive when done BY system.

## 2. Zeroth & First Laws
- **Zeroth law:** if A and B are each in thermal equilibrium with C, they are with each other → defines temperature.
- **First law:** ΔQ = ΔU + ΔW (energy conservation). ΔW = PΔV for gas.

## 3. Thermodynamic Processes
- **Isothermal** (T constant): ΔU = 0, ΔQ = ΔW = nRT ln(V₂/V₁). Slow process.
- **Adiabatic** (Q = 0): ΔU = −ΔW; PVᵞ = constant; TVᵞ⁻¹ = constant. Fast process.
- **Isobaric** (P constant): ΔW = PΔV; ΔQ = nCₚΔT.
- **Isochoric** (V constant): ΔW = 0; ΔQ = ΔU = nCᵥΔT.
- **Cyclic process:** ΔU = 0; net heat = net work = area of P–V loop.

## 4. Specific Heats of Gases
- Cₚ − Cᵥ = R (Mayer's relation). γ = Cₚ/Cᵥ (monatomic 1.67, diatomic 1.4).

## 5. Second Law & Heat Engines
- **Second law:** heat cannot flow spontaneously from cold to hot; no engine is 100% efficient.
- **Efficiency** η = W/Q₁ = 1 − Q₂/Q₁.
- **Carnot engine** (max efficiency): η = 1 − T₂/T₁ (temperatures in K).
- **Refrigerator** coefficient of performance β = Q₂/W.

## ⚠️ Common Traps
- In isothermal ΔU = 0; in adiabatic ΔQ = 0 — don't mix them up.
- Internal energy depends only on temperature (for ideal gas).
- Carnot efficiency uses ABSOLUTE temperatures.

## 📝 Quick Recap
- ΔQ = ΔU + ΔW (first law).
- Adiabatic: PVᵞ = const; Isothermal: PV = const.
- Carnot η = 1 − T₂/T₁.

## 🎯 Exam Tip
Identify the process FIRST (which quantity is constant), then pick the right formula — that's half the question solved.

notes.title: Kinetic Theory of Gases — Complete JEE Notes
notes.description:
## Overview
Microscopic model of gases linking molecular motion to pressure, temperature and energy.

## 1. Ideal Gas
- **Ideal gas equation:** PV = nRT = NkᵦT (R = 8.314 J/mol·K, kᵦ = 1.38×10⁻²³ J/K).
- Assumptions: point molecules, no intermolecular force, elastic collisions, random motion.

## 2. Pressure & Temperature
- Kinetic pressure: P = ⅓(N/V)m⟨v²⟩ = ⅓ρ⟨v²⟩.
- **Average KE per molecule** = (3/2)kᵦT → KE depends only on temperature.
- Temperature is a measure of average kinetic energy of molecules.

## 3. Molecular Speeds
- RMS speed v_rms = √(3RT/M) = √(3kᵦT/m).
- Average speed v_avg = √(8RT/πM); Most probable speed v_mp = √(2RT/M).
- Ratio v_rms : v_avg : v_mp = 1.73 : 1.60 : 1.41 (v_rms > v_avg > v_mp).

## 4. Degrees of Freedom & Equipartition
- Monatomic: 3 (translational); Diatomic: 5 (3 trans + 2 rot); Triatomic (non-linear): 6.
- **Law of equipartition:** each degree of freedom has energy ½kᵦT per molecule.
- Internal energy U = (f/2)nRT; Cᵥ = (f/2)R, Cₚ = (f/2 + 1)R, γ = 1 + 2/f.

## 5. Mean Free Path
- λ = 1/(√2 πd²n); average distance between successive collisions. λ ∝ T and ∝ 1/P.

## ⚠️ Common Traps
- v_rms ∝ √T and ∝ 1/√M — lighter gas molecules move faster.
- KE per molecule depends ONLY on T, not on gas type.
- γ depends on degrees of freedom (atomicity).

## 📝 Quick Recap
- PV = nRT; KE = (3/2)kᵦT.
- v_rms = √(3RT/M); v_rms > v_avg > v_mp.
- γ = 1 + 2/f.

## 🎯 Exam Tip
For 'compare speeds of two gases at same T', use v_rms ∝ 1/√M — heavier gas is slower.

notes.title: Oscillations (SHM) — Complete JEE Notes
notes.description:
## Overview
Simple Harmonic Motion: equations, energy, pendulum & spring systems, damping and resonance.

## 1. SHM Basics
- Condition: restoring force ∝ displacement, directed to mean position → F = −kx; a = −ω²x.
- **Displacement:** x = A sin(ωt + φ); A = amplitude, ω = angular frequency, φ = phase.
- **Time period** T = 2π/ω; **frequency** f = 1/T.

## 2. Velocity & Acceleration
- v = ω√(A² − x²); v_max = Aω (at mean position), v = 0 at extremes.
- a = −ω²x; |a|_max = Aω² (at extremes), a = 0 at mean position.
- Velocity leads displacement by 90°; acceleration is 180° out of phase with displacement.

## 3. Energy in SHM
- KE = ½mω²(A² − x²); PE = ½mω²x²; **Total E = ½mω²A² = constant**.
- KE max at mean position, PE max at extremes. Energy oscillates at twice the frequency of displacement.

## 4. Spring & Pendulum
- **Spring:** T = 2π√(m/k). Springs in series: 1/k = 1/k₁ + 1/k₂; parallel: k = k₁ + k₂.
- **Simple pendulum:** T = 2π√(L/g) (independent of mass and amplitude for small angles).
- **Seconds pendulum:** T = 2 s, L ≈ 1 m.

## 5. Damped, Forced & Resonance
- **Damped:** amplitude decreases with time due to friction.
- **Forced oscillation:** driven by external periodic force.
- **Resonance:** amplitude is maximum when driving frequency = natural frequency.

## ⚠️ Common Traps
- Velocity is max at MEAN position; acceleration is max at EXTREME position.
- Pendulum period is independent of mass.
- Energy frequency = 2× displacement frequency.

## 📝 Quick Recap
- a = −ω²x; v_max = Aω; a_max = Aω².
- T = 2π√(m/k) (spring), 2π√(L/g) (pendulum).
- Total energy = ½mω²A² (constant).

## 🎯 Exam Tip
Learn the phase relationships: at mean position KE & speed are max; at extremes PE & |acceleration| are max.

notes.title: Waves — Complete JEE Notes
notes.description:
## Overview
Mechanical waves, the wave equation, superposition, standing waves, beats and the Doppler effect.

## 1. Types & Wave Equation
- **Transverse** (particles ⟂ to wave, e.g. string) vs **Longitudinal** (particles ∥ to wave, e.g. sound).
- Wave: y = A sin(ωt − kx); ω = 2πf, k = 2π/λ.
- **Wave speed** v = fλ = ω/k.
- Speed on a string: v = √(T/μ) (μ = mass per length). Speed of sound (Newton–Laplace): v = √(γP/ρ).

## 2. Superposition & Interference
- Resultant displacement = sum of individual displacements.
- **Constructive** (path diff = nλ, in phase) and **destructive** (path diff = (n+½)λ, out of phase).

## 3. Standing (Stationary) Waves
- Formed by two identical waves travelling in opposite directions.
- **Nodes** (zero amplitude) and **antinodes** (max amplitude); distance between consecutive nodes = λ/2.
- **Stretched string** (both ends fixed): f = (n/2L)√(T/μ); fundamental n = 1. Harmonics: all integer multiples.
- **Closed organ pipe** (one end closed): f = (2n−1)v/4L → only ODD harmonics.
- **Open organ pipe:** f = nv/2L → all harmonics.

## 4. Beats
- Periodic variation in loudness when two close frequencies superpose.
- **Beat frequency = |f₁ − f₂|** (audible if difference ≤ ~10 Hz). Used to tune instruments.

## 5. Doppler Effect
- Apparent change in frequency due to relative motion of source/observer.
- f' = f(v ± v₀)/(v ∓ v_s): observer's speed v₀ (+ when approaching), source speed v_s (− when approaching).
- Does NOT apply when motion is perpendicular to line of sight.

## ⚠️ Common Traps
- Closed pipe gives only odd harmonics; its fundamental is HALF that of an open pipe of same length.
- Sound cannot travel in vacuum; light can.
- In Doppler, use correct signs — approaching raises pitch, receding lowers it.

## 📝 Quick Recap
- v = fλ; string v = √(T/μ).
- Closed pipe → odd harmonics; open pipe → all harmonics.
- Beat frequency = |f₁ − f₂|.

## 🎯 Exam Tip
For organ-pipe questions, first decide open vs closed — it changes which harmonics are allowed.
